title: What is a package
instruction:
Every Go file starts with `package`. Think of it as the file's team jersey — it tells Go which group this file belongs to.

`package main` is special: it's the team that runs the show. The `main` package is where `func main()` lives and the program starts.

Run this — it imports `fmt` and `strings`, two built-in packages. Imagine you're building a library catalog app: `fmt` prints your results, `strings` searches book titles.
successMessage: Two imports, two tools. `fmt` for printing, `strings` for cleaning up book titles. Go's standard library has dozens of these built-in packages — including `math` for stats and `time` for due dates.

title: Searching book titles with strings
instruction:
The `strings` package is your best friend when building a library catalog. You can search titles, count words, and clean up user input.

Useful functions:
- `strings.Contains(s, sub)` — does `s` contain `sub`?
- `strings.Count(s, sub)` — how many times does `sub` appear?
- `strings.ToUpper(s)` — uppercase
- `strings.ToLower(s)` — lowercase

Add two `fmt.Println` lines: use `strings.Contains` to check if `title` contains "Great", and `strings.Count` to count how many times "e" appears in `title`.

codeChecks.message: Use strings.Contains(title, "Great") to check if it contains "Great".
Use strings.Count(title, "e") to count occurrences of "e".
Replace the TODO comments with your code.

successMessage: Contains → true. Count → 3 (twice in "Great", once in "Gatsby"). The strings package has 20+ functions like these — perfect for searching through a library catalog.

title: The strings package for book titles
instruction:
The `strings` package handles almost every text operation a librarian needs.

Run this to see six common functions in action on book titles. Then add one more line: use `strings.ReplaceAll` to replace every space in `titles` with ` | ` and print the result.
expectedOutput: THE GREAT GATSBY TO KILL A MOCKINGBIRD 1984
The Great Gatsby To Kill A Mockingbird 1984
[the great gatsby to kill a mockingbird 1984]
the | great | gatsby | to | kill | a | mockingbird | 1984

codeChecks.message: Use strings.ReplaceAll(titles, " ", " | ") to replace spaces.
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: Seven functions, zero manual loops. You just formatted book titles like a librarian. The strings package is one of the most used in all of Go — keep it in your mental toolkit.

title: Library statistics with math
instruction:
The `math` package helps you calculate library stats — average pages, shelf space, membership growth.

`math.Pi` — the value of π
`math.Sqrt(x)` — square root
`math.Pow(x, y)` — x to the power y
`math.Abs(x)` — absolute value (negative differences)
`math.Round(x)` — round to nearest integer

Add three lines: print the square root of 144 (total members), 2 to the power of 10 (book copies after 10 doublings), and the absolute value of -7.5 (difference in late fees).

codeChecks.message: Print math.Sqrt(144).
Print math.Pow(2, 10).
Print math.Abs(-7.5).
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: √144=12 members, 2¹⁰=1024 book copies, |-7.5|=7.5 fee difference. The math package handles every standard operation — great for crunching library numbers.

title: Managing due dates with time
instruction:
The `time` package handles everything a librarian needs: due dates, borrowing periods, and overdue calculations.

`time.Now()` — current date/time
`time.Since(t)` — how long since `t`
`t.Format("2006-01-02")` — format a date (Go uses a reference date, not strftime)
`t.AddDate(y, m, d)` — add days to a date

Run this. Then add a line that prints today's date in `DD/MM/YYYY` format — like a due-date slip.

codeChecks.message: Use now.Format("02/01/2006") to print in DD/MM/YYYY format.
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: Go's time formatting is unique — instead of %d %m %Y, you use the reference date Jan 2 15:04:05 2006. Weird at first, but you'll never forget it. Great for printing due-date slips!

title: Library system info with os
instruction:
The `os` package lets your library system interact with the operating system: environment variables, command-line filters, file paths.

`os.Getenv("KEY")` — read an environment variable (e.g., `LIBRARY_PATH`)
`os.Args` — command-line arguments (like search terms passed by the user)
`os.Exit(1)` — exit with an error code

Run this. It reads the `HOME` environment variable (where the library config lives). Then add a line that prints how many arguments are in `os.Args` — useful for knowing if a book search term was passed.

codeChecks.message: Print the count: fmt.Println("Args count:", len(os.Args))
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: os.Args[0] is always the program name itself — so even with no extra arguments, len(os.Args) is 1. The os package is your bridge between Go and the computer running your library system.

title: Exported vs unexported
instruction:
In Go, whether a function or type is public or private is decided by a single rule:

**Capital first letter = exported (usable outside the package)**
**Lowercase first letter = unexported (private to the package)**

This applies to functions, types, variables, and struct fields.

`fmt.Println` is exported — `Println` starts with a capital P.
`strings.toUpper` would be unexported — but Go named it `ToUpper`.

Run this. Then fix the code by changing `book` to `Book` and struct fields to start with capitals — like any good library catalog entry.
hint: Change book to Book, title to Title, pages to Pages in both the struct definition and usage.

codeChecks.message: Capitalise the type name: type Book struct
Capitalise the field: Title string
Capitalise the field: Pages int
Capitalise the function: func Display(...)

successMessage: One rule, zero keywords. No public, no private, no protected — just the case of the first letter. Simple and consistent throughout every Go project ever written, including library catalog systems.

title: What is a module
instruction:
A module is a collection of Go packages with a shared identity and version — like a library's catalog system with multiple departments.

Every Go project has a `go.mod` file that defines:
- The module path (like `github.com/yourname/library-catalog`)
- The Go version
- External dependencies

You create one with: `go mod init module-name`

Run this. It reads `runtime.Version()` to show the Go version your code is running on — the same version recorded in go.mod.
successMessage: Your program knows what runtime it is on. The runtime package exposes this and more — goroutine counts, memory stats, CPU cores. The module system ties it all together into a versioned, shareable library project.

title: Using a third-party package
instruction:
Beyond the standard library, Go's ecosystem has thousands of packages at `pkg.go.dev` — like ISBN lookup, barcode scanning, or PDF generation.

You add one with:
```
go get github.com/some/package
```

This updates `go.mod` and creates `go.sum` (a lockfile with checksums).

For this tutorial environment we can only use the standard library — but the pattern is identical. Here we use `encoding/json` (standard library) the same way you would use any external package: import, call, done.

Run this. See how json.Marshal turns a Book struct into JSON — the same format a library API would return.
successMessage: A Go Book struct became JSON in three lines. This is how every library API serializes catalog data. The pattern — import, call, handle error — is identical whether it is a standard library package or a third-party one.

title: Import aliases
instruction:
You can give an imported package a different name using an alias:

```
import (
    str "strings"
    m   "math"
)
```

Then use `str.ToUpper()` and `m.Sqrt()` instead of the full names. Handy when building a library stats dashboard with multiple packages.

Aliases are also used to resolve naming conflicts when two packages have the same last segment.

Run this — it uses aliases for both `strings` and `math`. Then add a line using the `m` alias to print `math.Pi` rounded to 2 decimal places (for calculating library shelf area).

codeChecks.message: Use m.Pi (the math alias) to access Pi.
Format Pi to 2 decimal places with %.2f.
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: Aliases keep imports readable when names get long or conflict. In a library app with many dependencies, they make your code cleaner.

title: Blank import
instruction:
Sometimes you import a package purely for its side effects — it registers something when it loads, but you never call its functions directly.

`import _ "some/package"` — the blank identifier suppresses the "imported and not used" error.

The most common example: database drivers. `import _ "github.com/lib/pq"` registers PostgreSQL as a driver with `database/sql` without you calling anything from `pq` directly.

Run this. The `image/png` package is blank-imported to register the PNG decoder — like how a library might register a barcode scanner plugin.
expectedOutput: PNG support registered via blank import
successMessage: The blank import ran the png package's init() function — registering the PNG decoder — without polluting your code with unused identifiers. You will use this pattern to register database drivers or image format decoders.

title: Build a library catalog tool
instruction:
No starter code.

Build a small library catalog tool using `strings`, `fmt`, and `math`:

1. Start with this catalog entry: `"  The Great Gatsby by F. Scott Fitzgerald, 1925  "`
2. Trim the whitespace
3. Print the trimmed entry in UPPERCASE
4. Split it into words and print the word count
5. Print whether it contains `"Gatsby"`
6. Print the average word length rounded to 2 decimal places

Expected output:
```
THE GREAT GATSBY BY F. SCOTT FITZGERALD, 1925
Word count: 8
Contains Gatsby: true
Avg word length: 4.38
```
hint: For average length: loop through words, sum all len(word), divide by len(words). Use math.Round or %.2f for formatting.
expectedOutput: THE GREAT GATSBY BY F. SCOTT FITZGERALD, 1925

codeChecks.message: Use strings.TrimSpace to trim the whitespace.
Use strings.ToUpper to print in uppercase.
Use strings.Split to split into words.
Use strings.Contains to check for "Gatsby".
Use math package or %.2f to format the average to 2 decimal places.

successMessage: Five packages worth of functionality in under 20 lines. Entry trimmed, uppercased, split, searched, and analysed. The standard library does the heavy lifting — you just orchestrate it, like a librarian organizing a catalog.
